subject: Chemistry
q: Number of complexes which will exhibit synergic bonding amongst, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced open="[" close="]"><mrow><mi>Cr</mi><msub><mfenced><mi>CO</mi></mfenced><mn>6</mn></msub></mrow></mfenced><mo>,</mo><mfenced open="[" close="]"><mrow><mi>Mn</mi><msub><mfenced><mi>CO</mi></mfenced><mn>5</mn></msub></mrow></mfenced></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced open="[" close="]"><mrow><msub><mi>Mn</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><msub><mfenced><mi>CO</mi></mfenced><mn>10</mn></msub></mrow></mfenced></math> is
question: Number of complexes which will exhibit synergic bonding amongst, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced open="[" close="]"><mrow><mi>Cr</mi><msub><mfenced><mi>CO</mi></mfenced><mn>6</mn></msub></mrow></mfenced><mo>,</mo><mfenced open="[" close="]"><mrow><mi>Mn</mi><msub><mfenced><mi>CO</mi></mfenced><mn>5</mn></msub></mrow></mfenced></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced open="[" close="]"><mrow><msub><mi>Mn</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><msub><mfenced><mi>CO</mi></mfenced><mn>10</mn></msub></mrow></mfenced></math> is
solution:
Synergic bonding involves transference of electrons from ligands to metal. The transference of electrons takes place from filled metal orbitals to anti-bonding orbitals of ligands. It is a bond between a carbonyl group acting as a ligand and a metal. Synergic bonding means self strengthening bond. More the number of d electrons on the central metal ion more the synergic bonding. In metal carbonyls' oxidation state of the metal is zero.

In the complex MnCO5, the electronic configuration of Mn is 3d54s2. As carbon monoxide is a strong field, pairing of electrons occurs. Hence, the new electronic configuration of Mn is 3d7.

In the case of Mn2CO10 also electronic configuration after pairing of electrons is 3d7.

In the complex CrCO6 , the electronic configuration of Cr is 3d54s1 . As carbon monoxide is a strong field, pairing of electrons occurs. Hence, the new electronic configuration of Cr is 3d6

All of these compounds can show synergic bonding.

subject: Chemistry
q:
Number of paramagnetic complexes among the following is ________.
$[\text{MnBr}_4]^{2-}$, $[\text{NiCl}_4]^{2-}$, $[\text{Ni(CN)}_4]^{2-}$, $[\text{Ni(CO)}_4]$, $[\text{CoF}_6]^{3-}$, $[\text{Fe(CN)}_6]^{4-}$, $[\text{Mn(CN)}_6]^{3-}$, $[\text{Ti(CN)}_6]^{3-}$, $[\text{Cu(H}_2\text{O)}_6]^{2+}$, $[\text{Co(C}_2\text{O}_4\text{)}_3]^{3-}$
question:
Number of paramagnetic complexes among the following is ________.
$[\text{MnBr}_4]^{2-}$, $[\text{NiCl}_4]^{2-}$, $[\text{Ni(CN)}_4]^{2-}$, $[\text{Ni(CO)}_4]$, $[\text{CoF}_6]^{3-}$, $[\text{Fe(CN)}_6]^{4-}$, $[\text{Mn(CN)}_6]^{3-}$, $[\text{Ti(CN)}_6]^{3-}$, $[\text{Cu(H}_2\text{O)}_6]^{2+}$, $[\text{Co(C}_2\text{O}_4\text{)}_3]^{3-}$
solution:
To determine the magnetic nature of the given complexes, we analyze the oxidation state, electronic configuration, and the nature of the ligands for each central metal atom/ion:

1. $[\text{MnBr}_4]^{2-}$: Mn$^{2+}$ has a $3d^5$ configuration. Br$^-$ is a weak field ligand. The complex is tetrahedral with $5$ unpaired electrons. (Paramagnetic)

2. $[\text{NiCl}_4]^{2-}$: Ni$^{2+}$ has a $3d^8$ configuration. Cl$^-$ is a weak field ligand. The complex is tetrahedral with $2$ unpaired electrons. (Paramagnetic)

3. $[\text{Ni(CN)}_4]^{2-}$: Ni$^{2+}$ has a $3d^8$ configuration. CN$^-$ is a strong field ligand. The complex is square planar ($dsp^2$) with all electrons paired. (Diamagnetic)

4. $[\text{Ni(CO)}_4]$: Ni$^0$ has a $3d^8 4s^2$ configuration, which becomes $3d^{10}$ in the presence of the strong field ligand CO. The complex is tetrahedral with all electrons paired. (Diamagnetic)

5. $[\text{CoF}_6]^{3-}$: Co$^{3+}$ has a $3d^6$ configuration. F$^-$ is a weak field ligand. The complex is octahedral and high spin ($t_{2g}^4 e_g^2$) with $4$ unpaired electrons. (Paramagnetic)

6. $[\text{Fe(CN)}_6]^{4-}$: Fe$^{2+}$ has a $3d^6$ configuration. CN$^-$ is a strong field ligand. The complex is octahedral and low spin ($t_{2g}^6 e_g^0$) with all electrons paired. (Diamagnetic)

7. $[\text{Mn(CN)}_6]^{3-}$: Mn$^{3+}$ has a $3d^4$ configuration. CN$^-$ is a strong field ligand. The complex is octahedral and low spin ($t_{2g}^4 e_g^0$) with $2$ unpaired electrons. (Paramagnetic)

8. $[\text{Ti(CN)}_6]^{3-}$: Ti$^{3+}$ has a $3d^1$ configuration. The complex is octahedral ($t_{2g}^1 e_g^0$) with $1$ unpaired electron. (Paramagnetic)

9. $[\text{Cu(H}_2\text{O)}_6]^{2+}$: Cu$^{2+}$ has a $3d^9$ configuration. The complex is octahedral ($t_{2g}^6 e_g^3$) with $1$ unpaired electron. (Paramagnetic)

10. $[\text{Co(C}_2\text{O}_4\text{)}_3]^{3-}$: Co$^{3+}$ has a $3d^6$ configuration. Oxalate ($\text{C}_2\text{O}_4^{2-}$) acts as a strong field ligand for Co$^{3+}$. The complex is octahedral and low spin ($t_{2g}^6 e_g^0$) with all electrons paired. (Diamagnetic)

Therefore, the paramagnetic complexes are $[\text{MnBr}_4]^{2-}$, $[\text{NiCl}_4]^{2-}$, $[\text{CoF}_6]^{3-}$, $[\text{Mn(CN)}_6]^{3-}$, $[\text{Ti(CN)}_6]^{3-}$, and $[\text{Cu(H}_2\text{O)}_6]^{2+}$.

The total number of paramagnetic complexes is $6$.

Answer: $6$

solution:

q: The primary and secondary valencies of cobalt respectively in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced open="[" close="]"><mrow><mi>Co</mi><mo>(</mo><msub><mi>NH</mi><mn>3</mn></msub><msub><mo>)</mo><mn>5</mn></msub><mi>Cl</mi></mrow></mfenced><msub><mi>Cl</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> are:
question: The primary and secondary valencies of cobalt respectively in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced open="[" close="]"><mrow><mi>Co</mi><mo>(</mo><msub><mi>NH</mi><mn>3</mn></msub><msub><mo>)</mo><mn>5</mn></msub><mi>Cl</mi></mrow></mfenced><msub><mi>Cl</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> are:
solution:
Co(NH3)5ClCl2

The primary valency is the ionisable valency.  Oxidation number of Co is +3. So, primary valency is 3. Secondary valency refers the coordination number. It is an octahedral complex so, secondary valency is 6.

q: What is the spin-only magnetic moment value (B.M.) of a divalent metal ion with atomic number <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>25</mn><mo>,</mo></math> in its aqueous solution?
question: What is the spin-only magnetic moment value (B.M.) of a divalent metal ion with atomic number <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>25</mn><mo>,</mo></math> in its aqueous solution?
solution:
Electronic configuration of divalent metal ion having atomic number 25 is

Total number of unpaired electrons=5

μ (Magnetic moment) =n(n+2)BM

where n= number of unpaired e

∴μ=5(5+2)=35 B.M.=5.92 B.M.

subject: Chemistry
q: The compound used in the treatment of lead poisoning is:
question: The compound used in the treatment of lead poisoning is:
solution:
(1) Sodium calcium edetate, a salt of EDTA (Ethylene Diamine Tetra-acetic Acid), is used to bind metals in the chelation therapy, such as for treating mercury and lead poisoning.
(2) Cis-platin is used as an anti-cancer drug.
(3) D- penicillamine is used for treating copper poisoning.
(4) Desterrioxime B is used for treating iron poisoning.

subject: Chemistry
q:
Arrange the following coordination compounds in the increasing order of magnetic moments. (Atomic numbers: Mn=25;Fe=26)

A. FeF63-

B. FeCN63-

C. MnCl63- (high spin)

D. MnCN63-

Choose the correct answer from the options given below
question:
Arrange the following coordination compounds in the increasing order of magnetic moments. (Atomic numbers: Mn=25;Fe=26)

A. FeF63-

B. FeCN63-

C. MnCl63- (high spin)

D. MnCN63-

Choose the correct answer from the options given below
solution:
Magnetic moment is directly proportional to the number of unpaired in the central metal atom of a complex.

It is calculated by the formula, μ=n(n+2)BM

(A) FeF63-

Fe+3→3d54s0

n=5

(B) FeCN63-

Fe+3→3d54s0

n=1

(C) MnCl63-

Mn+3→3d44s0

n=4

(D) MnCN63-

Mn+3→3d44s0

n=2

Hence, correct order of magnetic moment is A>C>D>B

q:

(Given atomic number of Mn=25)
question:
The spin only magnetic moment of MnH2O62+ complexes is B.M. (Nearest integer)

(Given atomic number of Mn=25)
solution:
A spin-only magnetic moment occurs when an electron rotates on its own axis and generates a magnetic field.

μ = n ( n + 2 )BM
Here, n is the number of unpaired electrons.
The unit for a spin-only magnetic moment is the Bohr magneton (BM).

MnH2O62+

Mn2+=3d5

μ=5(5+2)=5.91BM

subject: Chemistry
q: The crystal field splitting energy of $\left[\mathrm{Co}(\text { oxalate })_{3}\right]^{3-}$ complex is ' $\mathrm{n}^{\prime}$ times that of the $\left[\mathrm{Cr}(\text { oxalate })_{3}\right]^{3-}$ complex. Here ' $\mathrm{n}^{\prime}$ is $\_\_\_\_$. (Assume $\Delta_{0} \gg \mathrm{P}$)
question: The crystal field splitting energy of $\left[\mathrm{Co}(\text { oxalate })_{3}\right]^{3-}$ complex is ' $\mathrm{n}^{\prime}$ times that of the $\left[\mathrm{Cr}(\text { oxalate })_{3}\right]^{3-}$ complex. Here ' $\mathrm{n}^{\prime}$ is $\_\_\_\_$. (Assume $\Delta_{0} \gg \mathrm{P}$)
solution:
The Crystal Field Stabilization Energy (CFSE) is calculated based on the electronic configuration of the central metal ion in the octahedral field.
For $[Co(ox)_3]^{3-}$:
Cobalt is in $+3$ oxidation state. $Co^{3+}$ has a $d^6$ configuration.
Given $\Delta_0 \gg P$, it is a low spin complex. The electrons fill the $t_{2g}$ level first.
Configuration: $t_{2g}^6 e_g^0$.
CFSE for $Co^{3+} = 6 \times (-0.4 \Delta_0) + 0 \times (0.6 \Delta_0) = -2.4 \Delta_0$.
For $[Cr(ox)_3]^{3-}$:
Chromium is in $+3$ oxidation state. $Cr^{3+}$ has a $d^3$ configuration.
Configuration: $t_{2g}^3 e_g^0$.
CFSE for $Cr^{3+} = 3 \times (-0.4 \Delta_0) + 0 \times (0.6 \Delta_0) = -1.2 \Delta_0$.
Comparing the magnitudes of the stabilization energies:
Magnitude of CFSE for $Co^{3+} = 2.4 \Delta_0$.
Magnitude of CFSE for $Cr^{3+} = 1.2 \Delta_0$.
The ratio $n = \frac{2.4 \Delta_0}{1.2 \Delta_0} = 2$.

subject: Chemistry
q:
Given below are two statements:
Statement I: Each electron in $e_g$ orbitals destabilizes the orbitals by $+0.6\Delta_o$ and each electron in the $t_{2g}$ orbitals stabilizes the orbitals by $-0.4\Delta_o$ in an octahedral field on the basis of crystal field theory.
Statement II: All the d-orbitals of the transition metals have the same energy in their free atomic state but when a complex is formed the ligands destroy the degeneracy of these orbitals on the basis of crystal field theory.
In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below
question:
Given below are two statements:
Statement I: Each electron in $e_g$ orbitals destabilizes the orbitals by $+0.6\Delta_o$ and each electron in the $t_{2g}$ orbitals stabilizes the orbitals by $-0.4\Delta_o$ in an octahedral field on the basis of crystal field theory.
Statement II: All the d-orbitals of the transition metals have the same energy in their free atomic state but when a complex is formed the ligands destroy the degeneracy of these orbitals on the basis of crystal field theory.
In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below
options: Both Statement I and Statement II are correct
Both Statement I and Statement II are incorrect
Statement I is correct but Statement II is incorrect
Statement I is incorrect but Statement II is correct
solution:
In an octahedral crystal field, the five degenerate d-orbitals split into two sets: a lower energy $t_{2g}$ set and a higher energy $e_g$ set.

The energy of the $t_{2g}$ orbitals is lowered by $0.4\Delta_o$ (stabilization of $-0.4\Delta_o$) and the energy of the $e_g$ orbitals is raised by $0.6\Delta_o$ (destabilization of $+0.6\Delta_o$) relative to the barycenter. Hence, Statement I is correct.

In the free atomic or ionic state, all five d-orbitals of a transition metal are degenerate (have the same energy). When ligands approach the central metal ion to form a complex, the spherically symmetrical field is disrupted, which destroys the degeneracy of the d-orbitals and causes them to split. Hence, Statement II is correct.

Both Statement I and Statement II are correct.

Answer: Both Statement I and Statement II are correct

solution:
The compounds having the same number of unpaired electrons will have same magnetic moment.

Magnetic moment μ = nn+2

where n is the number of unpaired electrons

[ Cr ( H 2 O ) 6 ] +2

C r 2+ .(3 d 4 )  with weak field ligand so unpaired electrons are 4

So, μ= 44+2 = 24

F e +2 ( 3 d 6 ) Weak field ligand so unpaired electrons are 4

μ = 44+2 = 24

q:
The correct order of energy of absorption for the following metal complexes is

A:Nien32+,B:NiNH362+,C:NiH2O62+
question:
The correct order of energy of absorption for the following metal complexes is

A:Nien32+,B:NiNH362+,C:NiH2O62+
solution:
Stronger the ligand, larger the splitting energy & higher the energy of absorption. Among the given options en is the strongest ligand due to ring formation than ammonia and than water. In all complex metal ion is same with same oxidation state. 

Nien3+2A>NiNH36+2B>NiH2O6+2C

subject: Chemistry
q: Which of the following complex will show largest splitting of d-orbitals?
question: Which of the following complex will show largest splitting of d-orbitals?
solution:
Some ligands are able to produce strong fields in which case, the splitting will be large whereas others produce weak fields and consequently result in a small splitting of d orbitals.

In general, ligands can be arranged in a series called spectrochemical series according to which maximum splitting is done by the strongest ligand which is cyanide (CN-) ion in this case.

intensity of colour of compound NiCl42->NiH2O62+>Ni(CN)42-

q: The secondary valency and the number of hydrogen bonded water molecule(s) in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>CuSO</mi><mn>4</mn></msub><mo>·</mo><mn>5</mn><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi></math>, respectively, are
question: The secondary valency and the number of hydrogen bonded water molecule(s) in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>CuSO</mi><mn>4</mn></msub><mo>·</mo><mn>5</mn><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi></math>, respectively, are
solution:
In CuSO4.5H2O, Cu is coordinated with 4 water molecules and two more oxygen atoms from Sulphate ion.

Now fifth water molecule is hydrogen-bonded and is deeply embedded in a crystal. It is not coordinated.

Only 4 water molecules are coordinated and the fifth is the only hydrogen bonded.

subject: Chemistry
q: A compound of vanadium chloride has spin only magnetic moment of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>73</mn></math> BM. Its formula is
question: A compound of vanadium chloride has spin only magnetic moment of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>73</mn></math> BM. Its formula is
solution:
Spin only Magnetic Moment

μ = n n + 2  BM = 1 . 7 3 = 3

∴  n = 1     (unpaired e-)

Electronic configuration of V= 1s 2 2s 2 2p 6 3s 2 3p 6 3d 3 4s 2



to get 1 unpaired electron V has to lose 4 electrons to form V 4+.

So, formula of chloride is VCl 4

(iii) MNH36n+
question:
Simplified absorption spectra of three complexes ((i) and (ii) and (iii)) of M+n ion are provided below; their λmax values are marked as A, B and C respectively. The correct match between the complexes and their λmax values is:

(i) M(NCS)6(-6+n)
(ii) MF6(-6+n)
(iii) MNH36n+
solution:
Stronger the ligand greater is splitting of d orbitals and smaller will be wave length of light absorbed.
The splitting power of ligands isNH3>NCS->F- So order of wave length of light absorbed is λNH3<λNCS¯<λF-

subject: Chemistry
q:
The correct order of the number of unpaired electrons in the given complexes is

(A) [Fe(CN)6]3-

(B) FeF63-

(C) CoF63-

(D) Cr( oxalate )33-

(E) Ni(CO)4

Choose the correct answer from the options given below:
question:
The correct order of the number of unpaired electrons in the given complexes is

(A) [Fe(CN)6]3-

(B) FeF63-

(C) CoF63-

(D) Cr( oxalate )33-

(E) Ni(CO)4

Choose the correct answer from the options given below:
solution:
The number of unpaired electrons in coordination complexes is determined by the nature of the central metal ion, the coordination number, and the identity and arrangement of the ligands around the metal ion. Based on these factors, the correct order of the number of unpaired electrons in the given complexes is:

A Fe(CN)63-:Fe3+3d5 (Pairing of electrons takes place) 1
B FeF63-:Fe3+3d5 5
C CoF63-:Co3+3d6 4
D Cr( oxalate )33-:Cr3+3d3 3
E Ni(CO)4 :Ni3d10(Pairing of electrons takes place) 0

No. of unpaired electrons order: E < A < D < C < B

subject: Chemistry
q: The wavelength of light absorbed for the following complexes are in the order \(\underset{\text{(I)}}{\left[\mathrm{Co}\left(\mathrm{NH}_3\right)_6\right]^{3+}} ;\underset{\text{(II)}}{\left[\mathrm{Co}\left(\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}\right)_6\right]^{3+}} ;\underset{\text{(III)}}{\left[\mathrm{Co}(\mathrm{CN})_6\right]^{3-}} ;\underset{\text{(IV)}}{\left[\mathrm{Co}\left(\mathrm{NH}_3\right)_5\left(\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}\right)\right]^{3+}} ;\underset{\text{(V)}}{\left[\mathrm{CoF}_6\right]^{3-}}\)
question: The wavelength of light absorbed for the following complexes are in the order \(\underset{\text{(I)}}{\left[\mathrm{Co}\left(\mathrm{NH}_3\right)_6\right]^{3+}} ;\underset{\text{(II)}}{\left[\mathrm{Co}\left(\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}\right)_6\right]^{3+}} ;\underset{\text{(III)}}{\left[\mathrm{Co}(\mathrm{CN})_6\right]^{3-}} ;\underset{\text{(IV)}}{\left[\mathrm{Co}\left(\mathrm{NH}_3\right)_5\left(\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}\right)\right]^{3+}} ;\underset{\text{(V)}}{\left[\mathrm{CoF}_6\right]^{3-}}\)
solution:
Wavelength of light absorbed increases as C.F.S.E of complex decreases.
$[Co(CN)_6]^{3-}$ has maximum CFSE
$[CoF_6]^{3-}$ has least CFSE
Ligand field strength $\uparrow$ ; C.F.S.E $\uparrow$
Correct wavelength order.
$V > II > IV > I > III$

subject: Chemistry
q: The calculated magnetic moments (spin only value) for species <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mfenced close="]" open="["><msub><mi>FeCl</mi><mn>4</mn></msub></mfenced><mrow><mn>2</mn><mo>-</mo></mrow></msup><mo>,</mo><msup><mfenced close="]" open="["><mrow><mi>Co</mi><msub><mfenced><mrow><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi><mn>4</mn></msub></mrow></mfenced><mn>3</mn></msub></mrow></mfenced><mrow><mn>3</mn><mo>-</mo></mrow></msup></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msubsup><mi>MnO</mi><mn>4</mn><mrow><mn>2</mn><mo>-</mo></mrow></msubsup></math> respectively are:
question: The calculated magnetic moments (spin only value) for species <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mfenced close="]" open="["><msub><mi>FeCl</mi><mn>4</mn></msub></mfenced><mrow><mn>2</mn><mo>-</mo></mrow></msup><mo>,</mo><msup><mfenced close="]" open="["><mrow><mi>Co</mi><msub><mfenced><mrow><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi><mn>4</mn></msub></mrow></mfenced><mn>3</mn></msub></mrow></mfenced><mrow><mn>3</mn><mo>-</mo></mrow></msup></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msubsup><mi>MnO</mi><mn>4</mn><mrow><mn>2</mn><mo>-</mo></mrow></msubsup></math> respectively are:
solution:
(i) FeCl42- Fe2+ 3d6→4 unpaired electrons.

μ=nn+2BM

=44+2BM

=24 BM⇒4.90 BM

 

(ii) CoC2O43-3 is a strong field ligand & causes
pairing & hence no. unpaired electron

μ=0

 

(iii) MnO42-  Mn+6 it has one unpaired electron.

Mn+6⇒Ar3 d1  μ=nn+2BM

=11+2BM

=3BM⇒1.73 BM
